title: Technical debt needs an interest rate
hook: Not every messy corner is urgent. The urgent debt is the part charging you interest every week.

beats.heading: Debt is not a synonym for code you dislike

blocks.html: Teams call everything debt: old code, ugly code, slow code, unfamiliar code. The useful version names the cost: slower delivery, more incidents, harder onboarding, or missed product options.

blocks.title: debt · cost log
blocks.caption: The useful signal is usually already there.

lines.t: checkout state machine: +2 days per pricing change
old admin CSS: annoying, low product impact
billing retry job: caused 3 incidents this quarter

blocks.caption: Leadership bugs are system bugs too. Pull context out and the team graph starts wobbling.

blocks.html: Attach debt work to a visible cost and a near-term opportunity. "Clean up billing" is weak. "Make pricing experiments one-day changes again" is a business case.

blocks.code:
| area | interest | payoff |
| billing retries | incidents | safer renewals |
| checkout state | slow changes | faster pricing tests |
blocks.caption: A debt register should read like a decision tool, not a shame list.

beats.heading: Pay down debt near the work

blocks.html: The best debt work often rides with feature work that already touches the risky area. Big cleanup seasons are harder to protect.

blocks.title: team clarity switchboard
blocks.caption: Flip the switches and watch the story turn into a tiny operating model.
blocks.low: muddy: people can move, but they are guessing while they do it
blocks.mid: clearer: ownership and tradeoffs are starting to show up
blocks.high: aligned: the team can act without decoding hidden context

blocks.html: When you can explain the interest rate, debt work stops sounding like polish and starts sounding like risk management.
